1:
On 1 Timothy 3:1 — “This is a true saying, If a man desire the office of a bishop, he desireth a good work” — the witnesses mark the worthiness of the pastoral office.

The Eastern fathers note the subject. Chrysostom: proceeding to the episcopal office, he “sets out with showing what sort of a person a Bishop ought to be.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “faithful is the saying” (pistos ho logos), pointing forward; “if a man seeketh” (oregetai), reach out after. Ellicott: a wish for high and arduous work, a noble one. Wesley: “an excellent, but laborious, employment.”

The verse opens the section on church officers. To desire the overseer’s office is to desire a good work. The ministry is a worthy aspiration, but a labor.

So the witnesses see Paul commend the pastoral office as a worthy object of desire. The witnesses note the formula “a true saying” — a faithful, trustworthy declaration, here introducing the subject of church officers. They mark the aspiration: “if a man desire the office of a bishop” — the overseer’s office, the eldership; to reach out after it is no unworthy ambition but, when rightly sought, a commendable longing. They observe the qualification implied: “he desireth a good work” — the office is a “work,” laborious and demanding, not a position of ease or honor merely; and it is a “good” work, excellent and useful. They note Wesley’s balance: an excellent but laborious employment. They observe that this introduces the qualifications to follow: precisely because the office is good and weighty, the man who fills it must be fit for it. The verse thus dignifies the ministry while sobering the aspirant: to seek the overseer’s office is to seek a good and noble work — and therefore one for which the candidate must be tested by the high standards Paul now lays down. The desire is honorable; the requirements, the following verses show, are searching.

So the witnesses see Paul commend the pastoral office as a worthy object of desire. The formula “a true saying” introduces the subject of church officers. The aspiration — “if a man desire the office of a bishop” — is no unworthy ambition but, rightly sought, a commendable longing. The qualification implied: “he desireth a good work” — the office is a laborious, demanding work, not ease or honour merely, yet excellent and useful. Precisely because it is good and weighty, the man who fills it must be fit; the desire is honourable, the requirements searching.
2:
On 1 Timothy 3:2 — “A bishop then must be blameless, the husband of one wife, vigilant, sober, of good behaviour, given to hospitality, apt to teach” — the witnesses mark the qualifications of the overseer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the list. Robertson: “the bishop” (ton episkopon), the overseer, used for the elders (Acts 20:28; Titus 1:7). Adam Clarke: episkopos, one with oversight. Barnes: “blameless” — a fair reputation. Wesley: “husband of one wife” neither requires marriage nor forbids a second.

The verse begins the catalogue of the overseer’s qualifications. He must be blameless, faithful in marriage, temperate, self-controlled, orderly, hospitable, and able to teach. Character and gift together fit him for the office.

So the witnesses see Paul set out the qualities required in the overseer. The witnesses note the foundation: “blameless” — not sinless, but of unblemished reputation, giving no just ground for accusation. They mark the much-discussed phrase “the husband of one wife”: the expositors generally take it to require marital fidelity and to exclude polygamy or scandalous remarriage — as Wesley notes, it neither commands that a bishop be married nor forbids a second lawful marriage, but requires that he be a man of one wife at a time, faithful in the marriage bond. They observe the personal virtues: “vigilant” (watchful, temperate), “sober” (sound-minded, prudent), “of good behaviour” (orderly, well-ordered in conduct); the social grace, “given to hospitality” (ready to receive and help strangers and the needy); and the one gift named, “apt to teach” — able and ready to instruct in sound doctrine, the distinctive requirement of the teaching office. They note that the list weighs character far more than gift: the overseer must above all be a man of proven, blameless life. The verse thus opens the standard for the ministry: a man of unblemished reputation, faithful, temperate, orderly, hospitable, and able to teach — the marks Paul will continue to detail.

So the witnesses see Paul set out the qualities required in the overseer. The foundation: “blameless” — not sinless, but of unblemished reputation. The much-discussed “husband of one wife” the expositors take to require marital fidelity and exclude polygamy, neither commanding marriage nor forbidding a second lawful one (Wesley). The personal virtues: “vigilant” (watchful), “sober” (prudent), “of good behaviour” (orderly); the social grace “given to hospitality”; and the one gift, “apt to teach.” The list weighs character far more than gift: the overseer must above all be a man of proven, blameless life.
3:
On 1 Timothy 3:3 — “Not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre; but patient, not a brawler, not covetous” — the witnesses mark the further moral qualifications.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “no brawler” (me paroinon), one who sits long beside his wine (Titus 1:7); “no striker” (me plekten); “gentle” (epieike). Adam Clarke: “not given to wine” also covers the imperious and abusive. Ellicott: a warning against one given to noisy banquets.

The verse adds the negative virtues. The overseer is not to be a drinker, violent, or greedy, but gentle, peaceable, and free from the love of money. Self-control and freedom from avarice mark him.

So the witnesses see Paul name the vices the overseer must be free from and the graces he must show. The witnesses note the prohibitions: “not given to wine” — not a tippler given to drinking, nor (as Clarke adds) the kind made imperious and abusive by it; “no striker” — not violent, not ready to come to blows; and “not greedy of filthy lucre” — not in the ministry for sordid gain, the love of dishonest money. They mark the answering graces: “patient” or gentle — forbearing, equitable, mild; “not a brawler” — not contentious or quarrelsome; and “not covetous” — free from the love of money. They observe Ellicott’s point that the warning against wine touches not only drunkenness but the frequenting of noisy banquets where rash words are spoken — conduct unbecoming the sacred office. They note that these qualities guard the overseer’s character against the besetting sins that would discredit his ministry: intemperance, violence, and greed. The verse thus continues the standard: the man of God must be temperate, gentle, peaceable, and free from avarice — a self-controlled life that adorns the doctrine he teaches, in contrast to the harsh, grasping, or quarrelsome spirit that would dishonor it.

So the witnesses see Paul name the vices the overseer must be free from and the graces he must show. The prohibitions: “not given to wine” (not a tippler, nor made imperious by it), “no striker” (not violent), “not greedy of filthy lucre” (not in ministry for sordid gain). The answering graces: “patient” or gentle, “not a brawler” (not quarrelsome), “not covetous.” Ellicott notes the warning against wine touches the frequenting of noisy banquets where rash words are spoken. The man of God must be temperate, gentle, peaceable, and free from avarice — a self-controlled life that adorns the doctrine he teaches.
4:
On 1 Timothy 3:4 — “One that ruleth well his own house, having his children in subjection with all gravity” — the witnesses mark the test of household government.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “ruling” (proistamenon), to stand before, govern (Romans 12:8); “in subjection” (en hypotage). Adam Clarke: governing the family with all gravity, not by tyranny. Ellicott: the life must be a pattern. Wesley: levity “undermines all domestic authority.”

The verse adds the domestic qualification. The overseer must govern his own household well, keeping his children respectful and orderly. Rule at home proves fitness to rule in the church.

So the witnesses see Paul make the well-ordered home a test of the overseer’s fitness. The witnesses note the requirement: “one that ruleth well his own house” — governing his family — wife, children, servants — wisely and worthily; not by harsh tyranny but, as Clarke notes, with proper authority and dignity. They mark the particular point: “having his children in subjection with all gravity” — keeping his children respectful, obedient, and well-behaved, and that with a seriousness and dignity that commands respect. They observe Wesley’s shrewd point that levity, light or undignified conduct, undermines all domestic authority; the overseer’s home must be marked by reverent order. They note that the principle is that a man’s government of his own household is the visible proof of his capacity to govern the household of God — the home a kind of training-ground and test for the church. They observe that this is no incidental matter but a real qualification: the candidate’s family life is to be examined. The verse thus adds the domestic credential: the overseer must show, by a well-ruled home and orderly children, the wisdom and gravity that fit him for the larger oversight — a point Paul drives home by the argument of the next verse.

So the witnesses see Paul make the well-ordered home a test of the overseer’s fitness. The requirement: “one that ruleth well his own house” — governing his family wisely, not by harsh tyranny but with authority and dignity (Clarke). The particular point: “having his children in subjection with all gravity” — keeping them respectful and orderly, with a seriousness that commands respect. Wesley’s shrewd note: levity undermines all domestic authority. The principle is that a man’s government of his own household is the visible proof of his capacity to govern the household of God — the home a training-ground and test for the church.
5:
On 1 Timothy 3:5 — “(For if a man know not how to rule his own house, how shall he take care of the church of God?)” — the witnesses mark the argument from the lesser to the greater.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reasoning. Robertson: “know not how to rule” (prostenai, know how, not merely that); a rhetorical question expecting “no.” Barnes: “a beautiful and striking argument” — a church resembles a family. Ellicott: the household is the test of a man’s capacity.

The verse gives the reasoning behind the domestic test. If a man cannot rule his own household, how will he care for God’s church? The smaller charge proves fitness for the greater.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the household qualification in a clear argument from the lesser to the greater. The witnesses note the logic: a man’s own house is a small, simpler charge; the church of God is a larger, weightier one. If he fails in the easier task — cannot wisely govern his own family — how shall he be trusted with the harder, the oversight of God’s church? They mark Barnes’s observation that this is a beautiful and striking argument: a church resembles a family, only larger and more varied, requiring the same skills of patient, wise, affectionate government. They observe the rhetorical force: the question expects the answer “he cannot” — failure at home disqualifies for the church. They note that the comparison both tests the candidate and dignifies the office: to “take care of the church of God” is a sacred stewardship, like a father’s care for his family but holier. They observe that the principle remains weighty: the ordering of one’s nearest relations is the proof of capacity for public trust. The verse thus seals the domestic qualification with reason: the well-ruled home is required because the inability to govern the smaller household betrays unfitness for the greater — the church of God.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the household qualification in an argument from the lesser to the greater. The logic: a man’s own house is a small, simpler charge; the church of God a larger, weightier one. If he fails in the easier task, how shall he be trusted with the harder? Barnes’s observation: a church resembles a family, only larger and more varied, requiring the same wise, affectionate government. The rhetorical question expects “he cannot” — failure at home disqualifies for the church. The ordering of one’s nearest relations is the proof of capacity for the sacred public trust.
6:
On 1 Timothy 3:6 — “Not a novice, lest being lifted up with pride he fall into the condemnation of the devil” — the witnesses mark the warning against the new convert.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “novice” (neophyton, newly-planted), our “neophyte”; “being puffed up” (typhotheis, from typhos, smoke, conceit). Ellicott: refers to want of experience. Wesley: “the condemnation of the devil” — “the same into which the devil fell.”

The verse warns against ordaining a recent convert. He must not be a novice, lest pride ruin him as it ruined the devil. Maturity guards against conceit.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the office against the peril of the immature convert. The witnesses note the requirement: “not a novice” — not a new convert, a “newly-planted” believer whose roots have not struck deep, lacking the experience and stability the office demands. They mark the danger: “lest being lifted up with pride” — the word pictures one inflated, swollen with conceit, as smoke puffs up; sudden elevation to high office may turn the head of one not seasoned in humility. They observe the dreadful consequence: “he fall into the condemnation of the devil” — understood, with Wesley, as the very condemnation into which the devil himself fell, namely through pride; the conceited novice would share Satan’s ruin, pride being the sin by which the devil was undone. They note the wisdom of the rule: the honor of office is a snare to the unproven, and a man must be tried and matured before being entrusted with it. The verse thus adds a vital safeguard: the overseer must not be a raw convert, lest the pride that office can breed in the immature cast him down into the very judgment that destroyed the devil — a warning that the office is to be filled by the seasoned and humble.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the office against the peril of the immature convert. The requirement: “not a novice” — not a newly-planted believer whose roots have not struck deep, lacking experience and stability. The danger: “lest being lifted up with pride” — swollen with conceit, as smoke puffs up; sudden elevation may turn the head of one not seasoned in humility. The dreadful consequence: “the condemnation of the devil” — the very condemnation through pride into which the devil fell (Wesley). The honour of office is a snare to the unproven; a man must be tried and matured before being entrusted with it.
7:
On 1 Timothy 3:7 — “Moreover he must have a good report of them which are without; lest he fall into reproach and the snare of the devil” — the witnesses mark the requirement of outward reputation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the concern. Robertson: “from them that are without” (apo ton exothen), outsiders — Paul’s care for the witness of outsiders (1 Thessalonians 4:12; Colossians 4:5); “snare” (pagida) of the devil. Barnes: a fair reputation among non-Christians. Wesley: lest they “rehearse his former life.”

The verse adds the witness of outsiders. The overseer must be well regarded even by those outside the church, lest disgrace and the devil’s snare overtake him. A good name beyond the church is required.

So the witnesses see Paul require that the overseer be of good repute even among non-Christians. The witnesses note the scope: “them which are without” — those outside the church, the heathen, infidels, and the world at large, who though they reject the faith can still recognize and respect an honest, upright life. They mark Paul’s consistent concern for the church’s witness before outsiders, that no ground be given for reproach. They observe the danger guarded against: “lest he fall into reproach” — lest his past or his conduct expose him, and through him the gospel, to scorn; “and the snare of the devil” — the trap by which Satan, through the shame and discouragement of disgrace, might entangle and overthrow him. They note Wesley’s point that a bad former reputation could be rehearsed by enemies to his hurt. They observe that this completes the overseer’s qualifications: not only blameless within the church and in his home, but of good name without — for a minister whose character the world can justly reproach dishonors his office and endangers his soul. The verse thus rounds out the standard: the overseer must bear a stainless reputation in the eyes of all, lest reproach become the devil’s snare.

So the witnesses see Paul require that the overseer be of good repute even among non-Christians. The scope: “them which are without” — those outside the church, who though they reject the faith can still respect an honest, upright life. The danger guarded against: “lest he fall into reproach” — lest his past or conduct expose him, and through him the gospel, to scorn; “and the snare of the devil” — the trap by which Satan, through the shame of disgrace, might overthrow him. A minister whose character the world can justly reproach dishonours his office; the overseer must bear a stainless reputation in the eyes of all.
8:
On 1 Timothy 3:8 — “Likewise must the deacons be grave, not doubletongued, not given to much wine, not greedy of filthy lucre” — the witnesses mark the qualifications of deacons.

The Eastern fathers note the order. Chrysostom: passing from bishops, he proceeds to deacons, omitting presbyters as “no great difference” from bishops. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “deacons” (diakonous), the second class of officers (Philippians 1:1); “grave” (semnous). The Puritan Henry: deacons had care of the church’s temporal concerns (Acts 6:2).

The verse turns to the deacons. They too must be dignified, sincere, temperate, and not greedy. The servants of the church’s temporal affairs require tested character.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down the qualifications for the second office, the deacons. The witnesses note the office: deacons, whose work (as Acts shows) was the care of the church’s temporal affairs — the relief of the poor and the serving of tables — that the ministers might give themselves to the word and prayer. They mark the “likewise”: the deacons, though not preachers, must be men of tested character, much as the overseers. They observe the qualities: “grave” — dignified, serious, worthy of respect; “not doubletongued” — not saying one thing to one and another to another, not deceitful or inconsistent in speech; “not given to much wine” — temperate, not addicted to drink; and “not greedy of filthy lucre” — not covetous of dishonest gain, a qualification especially needful in those who handle the church’s money and charity. They note Chrysostom’s observation on the silence about presbyters, taken to show their near identity with bishops. They observe that the deacon’s handling of funds and dealings with the needy make sincerity and freedom from greed especially vital. The verse thus opens the deacons’ standard: dignified, sincere, temperate, and honest men — fit to be entrusted with the church’s practical service and resources.

So the witnesses see Paul lay down the qualifications for the second office, the deacons. The office: whose work (Acts 6) was the care of the church’s temporal affairs — relieving the poor, serving tables — that the ministers might give themselves to the word and prayer. Though not preachers, they must be men of tested character: “grave” (dignified), “not doubletongued” (not deceitful or inconsistent in speech), “not given to much wine” (temperate), and “not greedy of filthy lucre” (a qualification especially needful in those who handle the church’s money and charity). Chrysostom notes the silence about presbyters shows their near identity with bishops.
9:
On 1 Timothy 3:9 — “Holding the mystery of the faith in a pure conscience” — the witnesses mark the inward requirement of the deacon.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Robertson: “the mystery of the faith” (to mysterion tes pisteos), the revelation given in Christ (1 Corinthians 2:7); “in a pure conscience” — “the casket in which the jewel is to be kept.” Barnes: hold firmly the great doctrines. Ellicott: mere orthodoxy without the Christ-life is empty. Wesley: “steadfast in faith, holy in heart and life.”

The verse adds the deacon’s spiritual qualification. He must hold the revealed faith with a pure conscience. Sound doctrine joined to a clean life is required.

So the witnesses see Paul require of the deacon both sound faith and a clean conscience. The witnesses note the doctrine: “the mystery of the faith” — the revealed truth of the gospel, once hidden but now made known in Christ; the deacon, though not a preacher, must firmly hold the great doctrines of the Christian religion, not be wavering or heretical. They mark the inward condition: “in a pure conscience” — the faith must be held not merely as correct opinion but with a clean conscience, a holy heart and life answering to the truth professed. They observe the beautiful image the expositors draw: the pure conscience is the casket in which the jewel of the faith is to be kept; sound doctrine must be lodged in a sincere and holy soul. They note Ellicott’s point that mere orthodoxy without the Christ-life is an empty thing; the deacon must have both. They observe that this guards against the divorce of creed and character: a man may hold right doctrine yet betray it by an unclean life. The verse thus completes the deacon’s inner qualification: he must hold the gospel mystery firmly and hold it in a pure conscience — orthodox in belief and holy in life, the truth kept in a clean vessel, before he is admitted to the office on trial.

So the witnesses see Paul require of the deacon both sound faith and a clean conscience. The doctrine: “the mystery of the faith” — the revealed gospel truth, once hidden, now made known in Christ; the deacon, though not a preacher, must firmly hold the great doctrines. The inward condition: “in a pure conscience” — the faith held not merely as correct opinion but with a holy heart and life. The expositors’ beautiful image: the pure conscience is the casket in which the jewel of the faith is kept. Mere orthodoxy without the Christ-life is empty (Ellicott); the deacon must have both, the truth kept in a clean vessel.
10:
On 1 Timothy 3:10 — “And let these also first be proved; then let them use the office of a deacon, being found blameless” — the witnesses mark the requirement of probation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the testing. Robertson: “first be proved” (dokimazesthosan proton), to test as metals (1 Thessalonians 2:4). Adam Clarke: let no man be put into office “till he has given full proof.” Ellicott: not a formal investigation but a trial over time. Wesley: “let a trial be made how they believe.”

The verse requires that deacons be tested before serving. Let them first be proved, then serve if found blameless. Office follows trial, not precedes it.

So the witnesses see Paul require that deacons be tested before being entrusted with the office. The witnesses note the principle: “let these also first be proved” — their character and qualifications are to be examined and tried, as metal is tested, before they are appointed; the same caution as for the overseer who must not be a novice. They mark that this proving is not, as Ellicott observes, necessarily a formal public investigation, but rather a period of observation and trial — letting their conduct, faith, and fitness be seen over time. They observe the order: “then let them use the office of a deacon” — only after they have been tested and approved; office is the reward of proven fitness, not a thing given to the untried. They note the condition: “being found blameless” — they must come through the trial without just charge against them. They observe Clarke’s point that no man should be put into church office until he has given full proof of sincerity and steadiness. They observe that this safeguards the church from entrusting its service to the unfit or the immature. The verse thus lays down the rule of probation: deacons, like overseers, are to be tested first and admitted only when found blameless — proven character preceding sacred office.

So the witnesses see Paul require that deacons be tested before being entrusted with the office. The principle: “let these also first be proved” — their character and qualifications examined and tried, as metal is tested. This proving is not, Ellicott observes, necessarily a formal public investigation, but a period of observation, letting their conduct and fitness be seen over time. The order: “then let them use the office” — only after they have been approved; office is the reward of proven fitness, not given to the untried. The condition: “being found blameless” — they must come through the trial without just charge against them.
11:
On 1 Timothy 3:11 — “Even so must their wives be grave, not slanderers, sober, faithful in all things” — the witnesses mark a verse of disputed reference.

The grammatical-historical commentators divide. Robertson: “women” (gynaikas) — apparently “women as deacons” (Romans 16:1 on Phoebe), not wives or women in general. Barnes notes Chrysostom and others take it of deaconesses. Adam Clarke: women in general. Ellicott: against the “wives” rendering. The genuine debate: deacons’ wives, deaconesses, or women generally.

The verse gives qualifications for “women” in the deacon context. They must be dignified, no slanderers, temperate, and faithful. Whether wives, deaconesses, or women generally is disputed.

So the witnesses approach a verse whose reference has long been debated. The witnesses note the qualifications required of these “women”: “grave” — dignified, serious, worthy of respect; “not slanderers” — not given to malicious gossip or backbiting (a besetting temptation guarded against); “sober” — temperate, self-controlled; and “faithful in all things” — trustworthy in every matter, whether toward God, husband, or the poor they serve. They mark the genuine debate over whom Paul means: some, including Chrysostom and Robertson, take “women” to mean women serving as deaconesses (like Phoebe), the verse’s placement amid the deacon-rules favoring this; others, with Clarke, take it of Christian women in general; and the older rendering “their wives” understands the wives of the deacons. They observe that the placement of the charge, set in the midst of the deacon qualifications, tells (as Ellicott notes) against the “wives” reading and toward an order of women in the church’s service. They note that, whichever is meant, the qualities required are clear and weighty. The verse thus sets a high standard for the women connected with the diaconal work: dignified, free from slander, temperate, and wholly trustworthy — fit to share in or support the church’s ministry.

So the witnesses approach a verse whose reference has long been debated. The qualifications required of these “women”: “grave” (dignified), “not slanderers” (not given to malicious gossip), “sober” (temperate), and “faithful in all things” (trustworthy in every matter). The debate over whom Paul means: some, with Chrysostom and Robertson, take “women” as deaconesses (like Phoebe), the placement amid the deacon-rules favouring this; others, with Clarke, as Christian women in general; the older rendering understands the deacons’ wives. Whichever is meant, the qualities are clear: dignified, free from slander, temperate, and wholly trustworthy.
12:
On 1 Timothy 3:12 — “Let the deacons be the husbands of one wife, ruling their children and their own houses well” — the witnesses mark the deacons’ domestic qualification.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel. Robertson: “of one wife” (mias gynaikos), at a time as in v. 2; “ruling well” (proistamenoi kalos) as in v. 4. Ellicott: the same orderly household required as for the presbyter. Adam Clarke refers back to the bishop’s qualification.

The verse applies the household standard to deacons. They are to be faithful in marriage and to govern their children and homes well. The same domestic test holds for the lower office.

So the witnesses see Paul require of the deacons the same domestic qualifications as of the overseers. The witnesses note the repetition: “the husbands of one wife” — faithful in the marriage bond, a man of one wife at a time, as required of the bishop; and “ruling their children and their own houses well” — governing their families wisely and worthily, keeping good order in the home. They mark that the standard for the deacon, though his office is lower than the overseer’s, is in these matters the same: marital fidelity and a well-ordered household. They observe Ellicott’s point that Paul looks for the same orderly, decorous home in the deacon as in the presiding elder — the domestic life being the proof of character for both. They note that the principle of verse 5 applies again: the man who governs his own house well shows the faithfulness and wisdom needed for the church’s service. They observe that this consistency underscores how seriously the apostle takes the home as the seedbed and test of fitness for office. The verse thus extends the household credential to the deacons: faithful husbands and good rulers of their families, proven in the home before serving the church — and such faithful service, the next verse promises, brings its own reward.

So the witnesses see Paul require of the deacons the same domestic qualifications as of the overseers. The repetition: “the husbands of one wife” — faithful in the marriage bond, a man of one wife at a time, as required of the bishop; and “ruling their children and their own houses well” — governing their families wisely, keeping good order. Ellicott’s point: Paul looks for the same orderly home in the deacon as in the presiding elder, the domestic life being the proof of character for both. The principle of verse 5 applies again: the man who governs his own house well shows the faithfulness needed for the church’s service.
13:
On 1 Timothy 3:13 — “For they that have used the office of a deacon well purchase to themselves a good degree, and great boldness in the faith which is in Christ Jesus” — the witnesses mark the reward of faithful service.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gains. Robertson: “gain to themselves” (heautois peripoiountai), lay by for themselves; “a good degree” (bathmon kalon). Adam Clarke: a “good degree” or step, possibly to a higher office. Wesley: “a good degree — or step, toward some higher office… much boldness, from a good conscience.”

The verse promises a twofold reward to faithful deacons. They gain for themselves a good standing and great confidence in the faith. Good service brings honor and assurance.

So the witnesses see Paul encourage faithful deacons with the fruit of their service. The witnesses note the principle: “they that have used the office of a deacon well” — those who serve with diligence, faithfulness, and sincerity — “purchase to themselves,” gain or lay up for themselves, a reward. They mark the first part of it: “a good degree” — a good standing or step; the expositors take this either as an honorable rank and esteem in the church, or, as Clarke and Wesley suggest, a step toward higher office, the well-proved deacon advancing in usefulness and trust. They observe the second part: “great boldness in the faith which is in Christ Jesus” — a holy confidence and assurance, both in professing and serving the faith, springing from the testimony of a good conscience; faithful service strengthens and emboldens the soul. They note that this is the encouragement set against the labor and risk of the office: diligent, honest service is not lost but rewarded with honor and inward boldness. They observe that the reward is moral and spiritual, not merely advancement. The verse thus crowns the deacons’ section with promise: faithful service wins a good standing and great confidence in the faith — an encouragement to serve the church well, which leads Paul to state the high purpose of all these instructions.

So the witnesses see Paul encourage faithful deacons with the fruit of their service. The principle: those who “use the office of a deacon well” — serving with diligence, faithfulness, and sincerity — “purchase to themselves” a reward. The first part: “a good degree” — an honourable standing in the church, or, as Clarke and Wesley suggest, a step toward higher office. The second: “great boldness in the faith” — a holy confidence and assurance springing from a good conscience; faithful service emboldens the soul. The reward is moral and spiritual: faithful service wins a good standing and great confidence in the faith.
14:
On 1 Timothy 3:14 — “These things write I unto thee, hoping to come unto thee shortly” — the witnesses mark the occasion of the letter.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the personal note. Robertson: “shortly” (en tachei) — “a pseudonymous writer would hardly have put in this phrase”; Paul’s hopes were not realized. The Puritan Henry: he wrote “the more briefly” expecting to come. Barnes: he hoped to finish the work in person.

The verse states why Paul writes. He sends these directions hoping to come soon. The letter is a stopgap for his expected presence.

So the witnesses see Paul explain the purpose of his written instructions. The witnesses note the immediate occasion: “these things write I unto thee” — the directions about the qualifications of overseers and deacons, and the conduct of the church; and the expectation: “hoping to come unto thee shortly” — Paul intended to return to Ephesus in person to complete and confirm the work. They mark Henry’s point that this hope of coming soon is why Paul wrote the more briefly, leaving fuller instruction for his expected visit. They observe Robertson’s telling note that the genuine, undesigned detail — the hope of a soon return that, as it happened, was not realized — bears the mark of authentic, occasional correspondence, such as a forger would not invent. They note that the letter thus serves as provision against delay: written guidance to stand in for the apostle’s presence until he can come. They observe the affectionate, pastoral tone — Paul writing to his trusted son in the faith, planning to be with him. The verse thus frames the letter’s function: a written charge sent in hope of a personal visit, which the next verse shows was meant to guide Timothy should that visit be delayed — that he might know how to conduct himself in the church of the living God.

So the witnesses see Paul explain the purpose of his written instructions. The occasion: “these things write I unto thee” — the directions about overseers and deacons; and the expectation: “hoping to come unto thee shortly” — Paul intended to return to Ephesus in person. Henry’s point: this hope is why Paul wrote the more briefly, leaving fuller instruction for the visit. Robertson’s telling note: the undesigned detail of a soon-return that was not realized bears the mark of authentic correspondence, such as a forger would not invent. The letter serves as provision against delay, standing in for the apostle’s presence.
15:
On 1 Timothy 3:15 — “But if I tarry long, that thou mayest know how thou oughtest to behave thyself in the house of God, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and ground of the truth” — the witnesses mark the great description of the church.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figures. Robertson: “if I tarry long” (ean bradyno); “how to behave” in the house of God. Adam Clarke: the “house of God” in allusion to the temple. Barnes: that Timothy might have written directions if the visit were delayed. The church is the pillar and ground of the truth.

The verse states the letter’s aim and exalts the church. If Paul is delayed, Timothy is to know how to conduct himself in God’s house — the church of the living God, the pillar and ground of the truth. The church is the truth’s support.

So the witnesses see Paul state the purpose of his instructions and rise to a lofty description of the church. The witnesses note the aim: should Paul be delayed (“if I tarry long”), Timothy must “know how to behave himself” — how to conduct himself and order the affairs of the church — for which the foregoing rules were given. They mark the three rich titles given to the church. First, “the house of God” — God’s household and dwelling, the family and temple where he is present, which must therefore be ordered with reverence. Second, “the church of the living God” — belonging to the true and living God, not a dead idol; a solemn dignity that demands holy conduct. Third, and most striking, “the pillar and ground of the truth” — the church is the support and bulwark of the truth, holding it up before the world as a pillar holds up a building, and grounding it firmly; the truth is committed to the church to uphold, guard, and display. They note that this exalted view of the church both motivates and dignifies the careful ordering Paul commands: because the church is God’s house and the truth’s pillar, its officers and conduct must be worthy. The verse thus crowns the section with the church’s glory: God’s living household, charged to uphold the truth — the truth whose great mystery the next verse confesses.
16:
On 1 Timothy 3:16 — “And without controversy great is the mystery of godliness: God was manifest in the flesh, justified in the Spirit, seen of angels, preached unto the Gentiles, believed on in the world, received up into glory” — the witnesses mark the great confession of the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the structure. Robertson: “without controversy” (homologoumenos), confessedly; “a great mystery” (Ephesians 5:32). Adam Clarke notes the textual question (“God” or “who” manifest). Wesley: “six articles… sum up the whole economy of Christ upon earth.” Ellicott: the glorious truth the church upholds.

The verse confesses the gospel in a six-fold hymn. Great beyond dispute is the mystery of godliness: Christ manifested in flesh, justified in the Spirit, seen of angels, preached to the nations, believed in the world, taken up in glory. The whole work of Christ is summed up.

So the witnesses see Paul crown the description of the church with the great confession it upholds. The witnesses note the emphatic opening: “without controversy” — confessedly, by universal agreement — “great is the mystery of godliness,” the profound, once-hidden truth now revealed, the very truth the church (v. 15) sustains. They mark the six-fold structure, likely an early hymn or creed, summing up the whole economy of Christ: “God was manifest in the flesh” — the incarnation, God revealed in human nature (the witnesses note the textual question whether the original read “God” or “who/which,” though the sense of the incarnate Christ stands either way); “justified in the Spirit” — vindicated and shown righteous by the Spirit, especially in his resurrection; “seen of angels” — beheld and wondered at by the heavenly host; “preached unto the Gentiles” — proclaimed to the nations of the world; “believed on in the world” — received by faith across the earth; and “received up into glory” — the ascension and exaltation to the Father’s right hand. They note that these six clauses trace Christ from incarnation to glorification, the sum of the gospel. They observe that this is the “mystery of godliness” the church is charged to hold up as the pillar of the truth. The verse thus closes the chapter with the heart of the faith: the great, confessed mystery of Christ — manifested, justified, seen, preached, believed, and glorified — the truth that the false teachers of the next chapter will assault.
